motion: Public campaign financing should replace most private fundraising.

turns.speaker: carlosramirez383
turns.content: I get why people like the idea of public campaign financing. It sounds cleaner, less corrupt, less tied to big donors. But realistically, it doesn’t remove influence—it just shifts it. Instead of private donors, now politicians depend on whoever controls the public funds. And you still have lobbying, independent spending, PACs… money doesn’t disappear, it just finds another route. If anything, you risk giving government more control over who gets visibility and who doesn’t.

turns.speaker: soopark524
turns.content: I think it is important to look at the core issue here, which is access and fairness. When campaigns rely heavily on private fundraising, candidates with wealthy networks have a structural advantage before voters even engage. Public financing does not eliminate influence entirely, but it reduces the imbalance. It creates a baseline where candidates can compete based on ideas rather than fundraising capacity.

turns.speaker: carlosramirez383
turns.content: But that assumes the system stays neutral. It rarely does. Whoever designs the rules decides how funds are distributed, and that opens a different kind of bias. Also, serious campaigns cost a lot. If public funding isn’t enough, candidates will still go looking for outside money. So now you’ve just layered systems on top of each other instead of fixing anything.

turns.speaker: soopark524
turns.content: That concern is valid, but it is more about implementation than principle. Poor design can undermine any system. The question is whether reducing dependency on private wealth improves democratic access. Evidence from smaller-scale implementations suggests it does. Even if not perfect, it moves the system closer to equity than the current model.

turns.speaker: carlosramirez383
turns.content: I’m not convinced it solves the real problem. Influence doesn’t disappear just because you change where the money comes from.

turns.speaker: soopark524
turns.content: It may not solve everything, but it meaningfully reduces one of the largest barriers to fair competition.

turns.speaker: kwamemensah631
turns.content: Let me put it this way. When you say you are choosing something, where does that choice come from? Your upbringing, your experiences, your environment… all of these shape you before you even act. So when the moment comes, you feel like you are deciding, but the path was already laid out. It is like a river—you think it is free, but it is following a channel that already exists.

turns.speaker: ashleydavis120
turns.content: I get what you’re saying, but it doesn’t fully land for me. Yeah, we’re influenced by a lot of stuff, obviously. But people still make decisions that go against how they were raised or what’s expected of them. You see people change careers, beliefs, lifestyles. That doesn’t feel like something completely predetermined.

turns.speaker: kwamemensah631
turns.content: Even those changes you are describing—where do they come from? New experiences, new information, internal reactions shaped by past events. It still follows a chain. You are not stepping outside the system; you are responding within it. That is the key point.

turns.speaker: ashleydavis120
turns.content: But if everything is just cause and effect, then what even counts as a choice? That definition makes the word kind of meaningless. At some point you have to acknowledge that humans experience agency, even if it’s not perfect or fully independent.

turns.speaker: kwamemensah631
turns.content: The feeling of choice is real, I agree. But feeling something and it being fundamentally true are not always the same. That is where the illusion comes in.

turns.speaker: ashleydavis120
turns.content: Maybe it’s not perfect freedom, but it’s still meaningful. And that’s enough for it to matter.

turns.speaker: omarhassan327
turns.content: This one is pretty straightforward to me. When the same families keep showing up in power, it limits who gets a real shot. It’s not always explicit, but the access, the name recognition, the networks—it stacks the system. And over time, that chips away at the idea that anyone can rise through merit.

turns.speaker: lucarossi551
turns.content: I think that’s a bit too absolute. Political families can also bring experience and continuity. Voters still have a choice at the end of the day. If a candidate wins, it is because they were selected, not simply because of their name.

turns.speaker: omarhassan327
turns.content: But that choice is influenced before the vote even happens. If one candidate starts with more visibility and connections, they are already ahead. It is not a level playing field.

turns.speaker: lucarossi551
turns.content: That imbalance exists in many areas, not just politics. The presence of advantage does not automatically invalidate outcomes. It just means the system needs safeguards.

turns.speaker: omarhassan327
turns.content: And dynasties make those safeguards harder to maintain.

turns.speaker: lucarossi551
turns.content: Or they simply reflect how voters respond to familiarity and perceived competence.

motion: Artificial general intelligence research should be globally regulated.

turns.speaker: nehagupta697
turns.content: If we look at this practically, AGI is not like regular software development. The scale of impact is much higher. So some level of coordination is necessary. Otherwise, different countries will push forward independently, and risks will not be managed properly.

turns.speaker: haochen471
turns.content: From a logical standpoint, global regulation sounds ideal but is difficult to enforce. Nations have different incentives and priorities. Even if agreements are made, compliance is uncertain. This weakens the effectiveness of such regulation.

turns.speaker: nehagupta697
turns.content: That is true, but lack of perfect enforcement does not mean no regulation should exist. Even partial agreements can create standards and slow unsafe development. It is about reducing risk, not eliminating it completely.

turns.speaker: haochen471
turns.content: However, introducing regulation too early may also slow beneficial innovation. There is a balance between safety and progress. Overregulation can have unintended consequences.

turns.speaker: nehagupta697
turns.content: Some structure is better than none. Especially when the stakes are this high.

turns.speaker: haochen471
turns.content: And that structure must be realistic, otherwise it risks being ineffective.

motion: Online anonymity does more harm than good.

turns.speaker: josemartinez17
turns.content: I’m gonna be honest, most of the time anonymity just makes people worse. People say things they wouldn’t say face to face, and it creates this environment where nobody feels accountable. Yeah, there are edge cases where it helps, but that’s not what most of the internet looks like day to day.

turns.speaker: priyasharma463
turns.content: I understand that point, but anonymity also protects individuals who cannot speak openly otherwise. For example, people in restrictive environments or vulnerable situations. So it is not only about negative behavior.

turns.speaker: josemartinez17
turns.content: I get that, but those cases are a small percentage compared to how often anonymity is used just to be toxic or spread nonsense. If the trade-off is overall worse behavior, then maybe it’s not worth it.

turns.speaker: priyasharma463
turns.content: But removing anonymity entirely could silence those same vulnerable voices. So the solution should focus on moderation and accountability mechanisms, not complete removal.

turns.speaker: josemartinez17
turns.content: I still think the net effect is negative. It just lowers the bar for how people act.

turns.speaker: priyasharma463
turns.content: It is imperfect, but it serves an important purpose that should not be ignored.
